entries.description: Pre-fill translations from a translation memory using exact and fuzzy matching.

downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.title: Downgrade Identical Exact Matches
downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.description: Reduce score by 1%% when multiple identical exact matches are returned

fillIfTargetIsEmpty.title: Only If Target Is Empty
fillIfTargetIsEmpty.description: Fill the target only when it has no existing content

fillTarget.title: Fill Target with Translation
fillTarget.description: Copy the best translation candidate into the target content

fillTargetThreshold.title: Fill Target Threshold
fillTargetThreshold.description: Minimum match score required to fill the target

makeTmx.title: Generate TMX Document
makeTmx.description: Create a TMX file with all leveraged matches

noQueryThreshold.title: No-Query Threshold
noQueryThreshold.description: Skip TM query if existing candidate scores at or above this value (101 = always query)

tmxPath.title: TMX Output Path
tmxPath.description: File path for the generated TMX document

doc.overview:
The **Recycle** tool queries a translation memory (TM) for each block's
source text and pre-fills the target with the best match. It tries an exact
match first, then a fuzzy match above a configurable threshold, recording the
match score and type (`exact` or `fuzzy`) on the block. It requires a target
language and a TM provider; without a TM the tool has nothing to leverage.

Whether a match is written into the target is governed by a fill threshold
and an "only if empty" option, so high-confidence matches can be applied while
weaker ones are recorded but left for review. A no-query threshold lets the
tool skip the TM lookup when an existing candidate already scores high enough.
Optionally, all leveraged matches can be written to a TMX file.

downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.description: Reduce the score when multiple identical exact matches exist.
downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.help:
When enabled, an exact match is scored 99 instead of 100 if the TM returns
multiple identical best matches, flagging the ambiguity for review.

fillIfTargetIsEmpty.description: Only fill the target when it has no existing content.
fillIfTargetIsEmpty.help:
When enabled, a match is written only if the block's target is currently
empty, so existing translations are never overwritten.

fillTarget.description: Copy the best match into the target.
fillTarget.help:
When enabled, the best qualifying match is written into the block's
target content. When disabled, matches are scored and recorded but the
target is not filled.

fillTargetThreshold.description: Minimum match score required to fill the target.
fillTargetThreshold.help:
A match must score at least this value to be written into the target.
Matches between the fuzzy threshold and this value are recorded but not
filled.

dependsOn.condition: filling must be enabled for this threshold to apply

fuzzyThreshold.description: Minimum score for a fuzzy match to be considered.
fuzzyThreshold.help:
The lowest fuzzy-match score (0-100) the TM lookup will return. Matches
below this score are ignored.

makeTmx.description: Generate a TMX document of leveraged matches.
makeTmx.help:
When enabled, all leveraged matches are written to a TMX file at the
configured output path.

dependsOn.condition: set the output path when generating TMX

noQueryThreshold.description: Skip the TM query when an existing candidate scores high enough.
noQueryThreshold.help:
If the block already carries a match score at or above this value, the TM
lookup is skipped. The default of 101 means the TM is always queried.

tmxPath.description: Output path for the generated TMX document.
tmxPath.help: File path where the TMX document is written when TMX generation is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: TMX generation must be enabled

limitations: Requires a target language and a TM provider; with no TM there is nothing to leverage.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with a non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Exact matches are tried before fuzzy matches; the match score and type are recorded as block properties.

With a structure-aware TM provider, matching runs against the block's inline-code structure and fills carry the matched entry's runs, so markup placeholders survive the fill; a 100% match means text and structure agree.
Ambiguous matches (several full-score entries with differing targets) are recorded but never fill the target — the choice surfaces for review instead of being picked by storage order.

examples.description: Fill the target from matches scoring 95 or above.

examples.title: Leverage and export to TMX
examples.description: Lower the fuzzy threshold and write matches to a TMX file.

entries.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing.

schema.title: Redact
schema.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing

toolMeta.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing

detectors.title: Detectors
detectors.description: Detection backends to run: rules and/or entities

rulesPath.title: Rules File
rulesPath.description: Path to a redaction rules YAML file

doc.overview:
The **Redact** tool replaces sensitive spans in the source with protected
placeholders before the content is processed (for example, before it is sent
to a translation provider), and stashes the originals locally so a later
`unredact` can restore them. It never emits the original value into the
rewritten content. It is a recoverable transformer: it produces an edit plan
— the span replacements plus the originals to vault — and the framework
applier rewrites the source, rebasing surviving run-anchored overlays so an
upstream annotator's terms or entities follow the rewrite.

Two detection backends can run, alone or together: `rules` matches literal
terms and regular expressions from a rules file or inline rules, and
`entities` redacts entity annotations already on the block (as produced by
`entity-extract`). When no detector is configured, `rules` is used. The
originals are stored on the block as an in-process secret annotation by
default, or written to a sidecar vault when a vault path is supplied (the
external mode used by `extract` → `merge`).

detectors.description: Detection backends to run: rules and/or entities.
detectors.help:
`rules` matches literal terms and regular expressions from a rules file or
inline rules. `entities` redacts entity annotations already attached to the
block (e.g. by `entity-extract`). Both may be listed; when none is set,
`rules` is used.

entityTypes.description: Entity categories to redact when the `entities` detector is enabled.
entityTypes.help:
Restricts entity redaction to the listed categories. When unset, the
default set is person, organization, product, and location — dates, times,
currencies, and measurements are intentionally excluded because they need
locale-specific formatting rather than hiding.
entityTypes.values: list of entity categories (default `[person, organization, product, location]`)

dependsOn.condition: the `entities` detector must be enabled

placeholder.description: Visible stand-in template for redacted spans.
placeholder.help:
The template used to render the visible placeholder that replaces each
redacted span. It supports `{category}` (the detected category) and `{n}`
(a per-category counter). When unset, the framework default template is
used.

rulesPath.description: Path to a redaction rules YAML file.
rulesPath.help:
Loads literal terms and regular expressions to redact. Only relevant when
the `rules` detector is enabled. A rules file may also define its own
placeholder template, which is used unless the tool's `placeholder` is set.

dependsOn.condition: the `rules` detector must be enabled

limitations: The `entities` detector only acts on entity annotations already present on the block; it does not detect entities itself — pair it with `entity-extract`.
Inline rules and the sidecar vault path are supplied programmatically by callers (e.g. extract → merge) rather than through the schema fields documented here.
Operates on translatable source spans; dates, times, currencies, and measurements are excluded from the default entity set.
processingNotes: A recoverable transformer — it returns an edit plan and the framework applier rewrites the source, rebasing surviving overlays across the rewrite. The placement pass requires it to run before any step that sends source to a remote sink.
Originals are recorded as an in-process secret annotation by default, or in a sidecar vault when a vault path is supplied (external mode); the applier vaults them atomically with the rewrite.
A later `unredact` reads the recovery record to restore the originals.

examples.description: Replace spans matched by a rules file with the default placeholder.

examples.description: Redact person and organization entities already annotated on the block.

entries.description: Remove target segments from blocks, by locale or by selected text-unit IDs.

filterByIDs.title: Filter by IDs
filterByIDs.description: When true filter text units by ID; when false filter by target locale

removeBlockIfEmpty.title: Remove Empty Text Units
removeBlockIfEmpty.description: Remove the text unit entirely if it has no remaining targets after removal

targetLocale.title: Target Locale
targetLocale.description: Target locale to remove (if empty all targets are removed)

textUnitIDs.title: Text Unit IDs for Removal
textUnitIDs.description: Comma-delimited list of text unit IDs whose targets should be removed; empty removes all targets

doc.overview:
The **Remove Target** tool deletes target segments from blocks. With a target
locale set, it removes only that locale's targets; with no locale, it removes
all targets. It is useful for resetting translations before a fresh run, or
for clearing targets in a specific locale.

Removal can be scoped to specific text units by ID, which restricts the
operation to a comma-separated list of block IDs. Optionally, a block whose
targets are all removed can itself be dropped from the stream.

filterByIDs.description: Filter text units by ID rather than by locale alone.
filterByIDs.help:
When enabled, removal is restricted to the blocks listed in
`textUnitIDs`. When disabled, removal is driven by the target locale
across all blocks.

removeBlockIfEmpty.description: Drop a block when it has no targets left.
removeBlockIfEmpty.help:
When enabled, a block that has no remaining target segments after removal
is dropped from the stream entirely. When disabled, the now-empty block is
kept.

targetLocale.description: The locale whose targets to remove.
targetLocale.help:
The locale to clear. When empty, the targets for every locale on the
block are removed.

textUnitIDs.description: Comma-separated text-unit IDs to restrict removal to.
textUnitIDs.help:
A comma-delimited list of block (text-unit) IDs. When set and ID
filtering is enabled, only blocks whose ID appears in the list have their
targets removed. Leave empty to remove targets from all blocks.

dependsOn.condition: ID filtering must be enabled for this list to apply

processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.

examples.title: Clear one locale's targets
examples.description: Remove the French targets from every block.

examples.title: Remove targets for selected text units
examples.description: Restrict removal to specific block IDs.

entries.description: Identify repeated source segments across blocks for translation-memory leverage.

doc.overview:
The **Repetition Analysis** tool tracks source text as blocks stream through
the pipeline and tags each block according to whether its source has been seen
before. The first time a given source text appears it is marked as a
first occurrence; subsequent identical occurrences are marked as repetitions.
Each block also records a group key linking equal segments, the running count
of occurrences, and its 1-based index within the group.

Source text is trimmed of surrounding whitespace before comparison. The output
feeds scoping and pricing: repeated segments can be translated once and reused,
so identifying them quantifies the leverage available from repetition.

caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, segments that differ only in letter case are treated as
distinct. Disable it to fold case differences together, so that, for
example, `Save` and `save` count as the same segment.

limitations: Matches exact (trimmed) source text only; near-duplicates and fuzzy matches are not detected here.
Repetition state is tracked across the whole run; ordering of occurrences depends on block order in the pipeline.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Counts the source side only.

examples.title: Case-insensitive repetition
examples.description: Treat segments that differ only in case as repetitions.

entries.description: Review each translation with an LLM, producing a score, assessment, and suggestion.

schema.title: Review
schema.description: Review translations with scoring using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Review translations with scoring using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

doc.overview:
The **Review** tool asks a configured LLM provider to review each
translation and return a concise assessment: a score from 1 to 10, a brief
judgement of accuracy and fluency, and a suggested improvement where warranted.
The review text is recorded on the block's properties along with the provider
name. Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.

Both a target locale and provider credentials are required, since the tool makes
an external API call per block. Unlike the AI QA check, which returns structured
issues, this tool produces a free-form review intended for human reviewers
deciding whether a translation needs revision.

apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the selected provider. Local providers such as Ollama may not
require a key. Prefer the credential store or environment over committing
keys to a recipe.

model.description: The model name to request from the provider.
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's default
model is used.

provider.description: The LLM provider backend to use.
provider.help: Selects which model backend performs the review.

limitations: Requires AI provider credentials and a target locale; makes an external API call per block.
The review is free-form model output, not structured QA issues.
Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks that have a translation for the target locale.
Blocks are processed in parallel by default for throughput.
The review text and provider name are stored on the block's properties.

examples.description: Score and assess each translation using Anthropic.

examples.title: Review with a specific model
examples.description: Use a named OpenAI model for the review.

entries.description: Classify each block into a scoping category from upstream repetition and match status.

doc.overview:
The **Scoping Report** tool assigns each translatable block a scoping category
and records it on the block's properties. The category is derived from
properties set by upstream analysis tools: a block flagged as a repetition by
repetition analysis becomes a repetition; blocks marked unchanged or leveraged
by diff-leverage become an exact match or a fuzzy match respectively; everything
else is classified as new.

Because it reads properties produced earlier in the pipeline, scoping report is
meant to run after repetition analysis and any leverage step. The classification
underpins word-count breakdowns and pricing that distinguish new text from
reused or partially matched text.
limitations: Relies on properties set by upstream tools (repetition analysis, diff-leverage); without them, blocks are classified as new.
Produces a per-block category, not an aggregated cross-document summary by itself.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Run after repetition analysis and leverage steps so their status properties are available.

examples.title: Classify blocks after repetition analysis
examples.description: The tool takes no parameters; place it downstream of repetition analysis in a flow.

entries.description: Run a user-supplied ES5 JavaScript script over each part to inspect or modify block text.

allowSourceMutation.title: Allow Source Mutation
allowSourceMutation.description: Permit the script to modify the source text (off by default; source is read-only)

doc.overview:
The **Script** tool runs a user-provided ES5 JavaScript program for every
part in the stream. The script receives the current part as a `part` object
and can read and modify block source and target text. It is an escape hatch
for bespoke transformations that no built-in tool covers.

Inside the script, `part.type` identifies the part and `part.block` exposes
the block (with `id`, `translatable`, `source`, and `targets`). Helper
functions are available: `emit(part)` forwards a part, `skip()` drops the
current part, and `log(msg)` writes a message to standard error. When the
script neither emits nor skips, the part passes through with any in-place
text edits applied. The script is provided inline or read from a `.js` file.

code.help: The script body, used when `source` is `inline`. Required in that mode.

dependsOn.condition: used when source is `inline`

scriptFile.help:
Path to a `.js` file to load and run, used when `source` is `file`.
Required in that mode.

dependsOn.condition: used when source is `file`

source.help:
`inline` runs the script supplied in the `code` field; `file` loads the
script from the path in `scriptFile`.

limitations: The engine supports ES5 JavaScript only.
Only block source and target text changes are applied back; other modifications to the part are not persisted.
processingNotes: Each tool instance owns its own runtime and runs single-threaded over its input.
A part passes through unchanged when the script neither emits a part nor calls skip.

examples.title: Trim trailing whitespace from source text
examples.description: Modify source text in place with an inline script.

examples.title: Load a script from a file
examples.description: Run a script stored alongside the project.

entries.description: Find and replace text in blocks, with optional regular-expression matching and flags.

dotAll.title: Dot Also Matches Line-Feed
dotAll.description: Make the period character match every character including line-feed

ignoreCase.title: Ignore Case Differences
ignoreCase.description: Ignore case when matching search patterns

multiLine.title: Multiline Mode
multiLine.description: Make ^ and $ match at the beginning and end of each line

regEx.title: Use Regular Expressions
regEx.description: Enable regular expression mode for all search patterns

replaceAll.title: Replace All Instances
replaceAll.description: Replace all matches instead of only the first

source.title: Replace in Source Content
source.description: Perform search and replace on source content

target.title: Replace in Target Content
target.description: Perform search and replace on target content

doc.overview:
The **Search and Replace** tool performs find-and-replace on block text. The
search/replace pairs themselves are supplied programmatically or by the
configured command; the schema-visible options here control matching
behaviour — literal versus regular-expression mode, case sensitivity, and
whether the replacement scans source, target, or both.

When regular-expression mode is on, the case, dot-all, and multiline flags are
applied to every pattern. By default the tool replaces all matches in target
text only.

dotAll.description: Let the dot metacharacter match line feeds.
dotAll.help:
When enabled (regex mode), the `.` metacharacter also matches newline
characters, so a pattern can span lines.

dependsOn.condition: applies in regular-expression mode

ignoreCase.help:
When enabled, matching ignores letter case. In regex mode this adds the
case-insensitive flag to each pattern.

multiLine.description: Make anchors match at each line boundary.
multiLine.help:
When enabled (regex mode), `^` and `$` match at the start and end of each
line rather than only the whole text.

dependsOn.condition: applies in regular-expression mode

regEx.description: Treat search patterns as regular expressions.
regEx.help:
When enabled, every search pattern is compiled as a regular expression
and the case, dot-all, and multiline flags below apply.

replaceAll.description: Replace every match rather than only the first.
replaceAll.help:
When enabled, all occurrences of a pattern are replaced. When disabled,
only the first match in each text is replaced.

source.help: When enabled, search and replace runs over the block's source text.

target.help:
When enabled, search and replace runs over the target text of the
configured locale.

limitations: Regular expressions use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround.
The search/replace pairs are supplied programmatically; only the matching options appear in the schema.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
When neither source nor target is set, the tool applies to both for backward compatibility.

entries.description: Count the source and target segments in each translatable block.

doc.overview:
The **Segment Count** tool records how many segments each translatable block
contains. The source segment count is always recorded on the block's
properties. When a target locale is configured and the block has a translation
for it, the number of target segments is recorded as well.

Counting segments — rather than words or characters — measures the granularity
of a block after segmentation, which is useful for verifying that source and
target segmentation line up and for segment-based scoping.
limitations: Target segment counts require a configured target locale and an existing translation for the block.
Counts follow the active segmentation; results depend on how the text was segmented.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.

examples.title: Count segments
examples.description: The tool takes no parameters; add it to a flow with an empty configuration.

schema.title: Segmentation
schema.description: Split source text into sentence or chunk segments (stand-off overlay)

engine.title: Segmentation Engine
engine.description: Which segmenter to use; the default needs no configuration

ui:enum-descriptions.srx: Faithful sentence segmentation: ICU UAX-29 boundaries refined by Okapi SRX exceptions where ICU is available, pure-Go SRX rules otherwise. No configuration required.
ui:enum-descriptions.uax29: Unicode default sentence boundaries (ICU UAX-29). A language-agnostic baseline with no exceptions.

maxChunkRunes.description: Soft upper bound on chunk size in characters (0 = model default)

renumberCodes.description: Renumber inline code IDs when materializing segments to a bilingual format

rulesPath.description: Path to a custom SRX 2.0 rules file (overrides the built-in ruleset)

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace during segmentation

trimLeadingWhitespace.title: Trim Leading Whitespace
trimLeadingWhitespace.description: Exclude leading whitespace from each segment span

trimTrailingWhitespace.title: Trim Trailing Whitespace
trimTrailingWhitespace.description: Exclude trailing whitespace from each segment span

doc.overview:
The **Segmentation** tool splits a block's text into sentence-level segments.
Segmentation determines the unit of translation and of translation-memory
matching, so consistent segmentation is important for leverage and review.
By default the tool segments source text using built-in SRX-style rules that
handle common sentence boundaries and abbreviations; rules can also be loaded
from an SRX file.

The number of segments produced is recorded on each block. Already-segmented
text is left alone unless re-segmentation is requested. Target text can be
segmented independently, with its own rules file.

overwriteSegmentation.help:
By default text that already has more than one segment is left untouched.
Enable this to discard the existing segmentation and re-segment.

renumberCodes.description: Renumber inline-code IDs within each segment.
renumberCodes.help:
When enabled, inline-code IDs are renumbered to start at 1 within each
resulting segment.

segmentSource.help:
When enabled, the source text is split into segments using the source
rules. If neither source nor target segmentation is enabled, source
segmentation is applied by default.

segmentTarget.help:
When enabled, existing target text for the configured locale is split
into segments using the target rules.

sourceSrxPath.description: Path to an SRX rules file for the source.
sourceSrxPath.help:
Path to an SRX segmentation rules file used for the source text. When
empty, the built-in default rules are used.

targetSrxPath.description: Path to an SRX rules file for the target.
targetSrxPath.help:
Path to an SRX segmentation rules file used for the target text. When
empty, the built-in default rules are used.

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace while segmenting.
treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.help:
When enabled, an isolated inline code is treated like whitespace for the
purpose of finding segment boundaries.

limitations: The built-in rule set targets common Latin-script sentence boundaries; non-Latin scripts may need a custom SRX file.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
The resulting segment count is written to a block property.

examples.description: Split source into sentences using the built-in rules.

blockSeverity.title: Blocking severity
blockSeverity.description: Lowest finding severity that keeps a block from being marked checked (minor|major|critical)

entries.description: Reclassify generic markup inline codes into semantic vocabulary types.

doc.overview:
The **Span Classify** tool reclassifies generic `code:markup` inline codes —
placeholders and paired open/close codes — into proper semantic vocabulary
types such as bold, italic, hyperlink, or image. It is designed to run after
readers that emit generic markup codes (for example bridge XLIFF or Okapi
readers), so that later tools and editors see meaningful inline-code types
rather than opaque markup.

Classification uses two strategies: a map of known Okapi code sub-types, and,
failing that, the HTML element name parsed from the code's raw data. When a
semantic type is resolved, the tool also applies the vocabulary's display
form, equivalence text, and editing constraints. Codes it cannot classify are
left as they are. The tool has no configurable parameters.
limitations: Classification covers known Okapi sub-types and common HTML elements; unrecognized codes are left unchanged.
Has no configurable parameters.
processingNotes: Operates on inline-code runs in both source and target segments.
Recurses into plural and select constructs so codes inside them are classified as well.

entries.description: Identify tags and placeholders in source text and mark them for downstream protection.

patterns.title: Protection Patterns
patterns.description: Regex patterns for tags and placeholders to protect

doc.overview:
The **Tag Protect** tool finds tags and placeholders in source text using
regular-expression patterns and records them as an annotation on the block,
along with a count property. Downstream tools — for example an MT or AI
translation step — can then read the annotation to preserve those tokens
rather than translating or reformatting them.

When no patterns are configured, a built-in default set is used, covering
HTML/XML tags, curly-brace placeholders (ICU, Java, .NET), printf-style
placeholders, and `${...}` template expressions. The tool records what it
found; preserving the matches is the job of the consuming tool.

patterns.description: Regular-expression patterns for tags and placeholders to protect.
patterns.help:
A list of regular expressions; any substring of the source matching a
pattern is recorded as a protected tag. When the list is empty, a
built-in default set is used (HTML/XML tags, curly-brace placeholders,
printf-style placeholders, and `${...}` template expressions).
patterns.values: array of regular expressions (default: built-in set)

limitations: Patterns use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround.
The tool records protected matches as an annotation; it does not itself prevent their modification.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Matches are recorded as a block annotation and the match count as a block property; matches are deduplicated and ordered by offset.

examples.title: Protect the default token set
examples.description: Use built-in patterns for tags and common placeholders.

examples.title: Protect only HTML tags
examples.description: Restrict protection to markup tags.

entries.description: Verify that required target terms appear when their source terms are present.

doc.overview:
The **Terminology Check** tool checks each translation against a glossary of
source/target term pairs. For every entry, if the source term occurs in the
block's source text, the tool verifies that the required target term occurs in
the translation; when it does not, the block is flagged and the missing
expectations are recorded on its properties. A target locale is required, and a
termbase or glossary supplies the term pairs.

The glossary is supplied programmatically rather than as a schema field, so the
tool is typically wired up by the host application or a project that binds a
termbase. The only schema-level option controls whether term matching respects
letter case.

caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, source and target terms must match exactly, including letter
case. When disabled, matching is case-insensitive, so a glossary term
matches regardless of how it is capitalised in the text.

limitations: The glossary of term pairs is configured programmatically; it is not exposed as a schema parameter.
Matching is substring-based; it does not account for inflection or morphology.
Requires a configured target locale and a termbase; blocks without a translation are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Only entries whose source term is present in the block's source are checked.

examples.title: Case-insensitive term matching
examples.description: Match glossary terms regardless of capitalisation.

entries.description: Translate block content with an LLM or machine-translation provider.

schema.title: Translate
schema.description: Translate content with an LLM or machine-translation provider (select an engine, then a provider)

toolMeta.description: Translate content with an LLM or machine-translation provider (select an engine, then a provider)

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

batchConcurrency.title: Batch Concurrency
batchConcurrency.description: Number of concurrent batch calls (0 or 1 = sequential)

batchSize.title: Batch Size
batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call

email.title: Email
email.description: Account email for higher rate limits (mymemory)

engine.title: Engine
engine.description: Translation engine: an AI model or a machine-translation provider

ui:enum-descriptions.llm: Translate with a large language model.
ui:enum-descriptions.mt: Translate with a dedicated machine-translation provider.

skipMatched.title: Skip Matched
skipMatched.description: Skip blocks that already have a target translation

doc.overview:
The **Translate** tool translates untranslated block content. A single
`--provider` selects the backend: a large-language-model provider (Anthropic,
OpenAI, Gemini, Azure OpenAI, Ollama) or a machine-translation engine (DeepL,
Google, Microsoft, ModernMT, MyMemory). LLM and MT engines are not separate
commands — they are values of `--provider`. The result is written as the target
text, with an alternate-translation annotation recording the originating
provider. A target language is required, and credentials are required for
hosted providers (local Ollama and the offline demo need none).

Blocks that contain inline codes are sent through a placeholder-preserving
prompt so tags survive translation, then reconstructed from the response.
Blocks can be translated one at a time or grouped into batches sent in a
single structured call; batches can run concurrently. When run with a session
cache, already-translated targets are reused and fresh translations are
written back, so repeat runs skip work already done.

apiKey.help:
Credential for the chosen hosted LLM provider or MT engine. Required for
hosted providers; not needed for a local Ollama backend.

batchConcurrency.description: Number of batch calls to run in parallel.
batchConcurrency.help:
How many batches are translated concurrently. A value of 0 or 1 runs
batches sequentially.

batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call.
batchSize.help:
Blocks are grouped into batches of this size and translated in a single
structured call per batch (LLM providers). A value of 1 translates block by
block.

email.description: Account email for higher rate limits (MyMemory).
email.help: An optional MyMemory account email that raises the engine's rate limit.

model.description: The model name to request (LLM providers).
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier for an LLM provider. When omitted,
the provider's default model is used. Ignored by MT engines.

projectId.help: The Google Cloud project id used by the `google` engine.

provider.help:
Selects the backend. LLM providers (`anthropic`, `openai`, `gemini`,
`azureopenai`, `ollama`) and MT engines (`deepl`, `google`, `microsoft`,
`modernmt`, `mymemory`) are all chosen here. Hosted providers require an
API key; Ollama runs locally. The available options are populated from the
provider registry.

region.help: The Azure region for the `microsoft` engine.

skipMatched.description: Skip blocks that already have a target translation.
skipMatched.help:
When enabled, a block that already has a target for the locale is left
untouched, so only untranslated content is sent to the provider.

subscriptionKey.help: The Azure subscription key used by the `microsoft` engine.

limitations: Requires a target language and, for hosted providers, valid credentials.
Hosted providers make network API calls; cost and latency scale with content volume.
Session-cache reuse applies on the block-by-block path; the batched path writes overlays on output but does not pre-skip via the per-block cache.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Blocks with inline codes are translated through a placeholder-preserving prompt and reconstructed from the response.
Each translation is recorded as an alternate-translation annotation tagged with the provider.

entries.description: Compare two target translations of the same source and report whether they match.

caseSensitive.title: Case Sensitive
caseSensitive.description: Take case differences into account when comparing

document1Label.title: Document 1 Label
document1Label.description: Label for the first translation in reports

document2Label.title: Document 2 Label
document2Label.description: Label for the second translation in reports

genericCodes.title: Use Generic Codes
genericCodes.description: Use generic numbered tags (e.g. <1>...</1>) instead of original inline codes in reports

punctuationSensitive.title: Punctuation Sensitive
punctuationSensitive.description: Take punctuation differences into account when comparing

whitespaceSensitive.title: Whitespace Sensitive
whitespaceSensitive.description: Take whitespace differences into account when comparing

doc.overview:
The **Translation Comparison** tool compares the translations of a block in two
target locales (or two versions) and records the result on the block's
properties: a status — identical, different, or one of the missing-translation
cases — and a human-readable diff description. When a translation is absent for
one or both locales, the status reflects which side is missing.

Comparison sensitivity is configurable: case, whitespace, and punctuation can
each be ignored so that cosmetic differences do not register as a mismatch. The
two locales being compared are supplied programmatically; the parameters below
control how the comparison is performed and how the translations are labelled
in the report.

caseSensitive.description: Take case differences into account when comparing.
caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, translations that differ only in letter case count as
different. Disable it to compare case-insensitively.

document1Label.description: Label for the first translation in reports.
document1Label.help:
The name used to identify the first translation in the diff description. If
left empty, the first locale's identifier is used instead.

document2Label.description: Label for the second translation in reports.
document2Label.help:
The name used to identify the second translation in the diff description. If
left empty, the second locale's identifier is used instead.

genericCodes.description: Use generic numbered tags instead of original inline codes in reports.
genericCodes.help:
When enabled, inline codes are represented in the report as generic numbered
tags (for example `<1>...</1>`) rather than their original markup, which
keeps the diff readable.

punctuationSensitive.description: Take punctuation differences into account when comparing.
punctuationSensitive.help:
When enabled, punctuation differences count as a mismatch. When disabled,
punctuation characters are stripped from both sides before comparison.

whitespaceSensitive.description: Take whitespace differences into account when comparing.
whitespaceSensitive.help:
When enabled, differences in whitespace count as a mismatch. When disabled,
runs of whitespace are collapsed to a single space and trimmed before
comparison, so spacing differences are ignored.

limitations: Compares two target locales of the same block; it does not compare against an external reference file.
A block is only compared when it carries translations for the configured locales; otherwise a missing-translation status is recorded.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
The two locales to compare are configured programmatically, not through the parameters above.

examples.title: Ignore cosmetic differences
examples.description: Treat translations that differ only in case, whitespace, or punctuation as identical.
examples.config:
caseSensitive: false
whitespaceSensitive: false
punctuationSensitive: false

examples.title: Label the compared translations
examples.description: Use meaningful labels for the two translations in the diff output.
examples.config:
document1Label: Vendor A
document2Label: Vendor B

entries.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing.

schema.title: Unredact
schema.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing

toolMeta.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing

doc.overview:
The **Unredact** tool reverses `redact`: it restores the original values into
the redaction placeholders across a block's source and target runs, then
removes the in-process secret annotation so nothing sensitive reaches the
writer. It is the closing half of the redact → process → unredact cycle, run
after the protected content has been processed (for example, translated).

Restoration uses the recovery record left by `redact`. By default it reads the
in-process secret annotation on the block; in external mode it reads originals
from a sidecar vault (used by the `extract` → `merge` workflow). Both an
ID-based restore (for structure-preserving formats and inline codes) and a
text-based restore (for formats that flattened a placeholder to its visible
string on write) are applied, so placeholders are recovered in either case.
The vault path is supplied programmatically by the caller rather than as a
schema field, so this tool exposes no schema parameters.
limitations: Exposes no schema parameters; the optional sidecar vault path is supplied programmatically by callers (e.g. extract → merge).
Restores only placeholders recorded by a preceding `redact` run; there is nothing to restore without that recovery record.
processingNotes: A transformer that restores originals into both source and target runs — it returns an edit plan and the framework applier rewrites the block, rebasing surviving source overlays across the restore.
Reads the in-process secret annotation by default, or a sidecar vault in external mode.
Removes the secret annotation after restoring, so sensitive originals never reach the writer.

entries.description: Encode or decode URI percent-escape sequences in block text.

doc.overview:
The **URI Convert** tool encodes or decodes URI percent-escape sequences in
block text. Decoding turns `%XX` escapes back into their characters; encoding
replaces reserved characters with percent escapes. Use it to make URL-encoded
content readable for translation, or to re-encode it for output.

By default the tool decodes the target text for the configured locale; a
target locale is required when applying to the target.

applySource.description: Apply the conversion to source text.
applySource.help: When enabled, the source text is encoded or decoded.

applyTarget.description: Apply the conversion to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is converted for the configured locale.
Only existing targets are touched.

dependsOn.condition: required when applyTarget is enabled

mode.help:
`decode` converts `%XX` escape sequences back to their characters;
`encode` replaces reserved characters with percent escapes.

targetLocale.help: The locale whose target text is converted. Required when `applyTarget` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Conversion uses path-style escaping; decoding malformed escape sequences fails the run.

examples.title: Decode percent escapes in the target
examples.description: Make URL-encoded target text readable.
examples.config:
mode: decode
applyTarget: true
targetLocale: fr-FR

examples.title: Encode source text
examples.description: Percent-encode reserved characters in the source.

entries.description: Normalize and fix whitespace issues in target translations, including punctuation spacing.

includeHorizontalWS.title: Horizontal Tabs
includeHorizontalWS.description: Include horizontal tab characters in corrections

includeVerticalWS.title: Vertical White Space
includeVerticalWS.description: Include vertical whitespace (line feeds and carriage returns) in corrections

matchSourceWhitespace.title: Match Source Whitespace
matchSourceWhitespace.description: Copy source leading/trailing whitespace to target

normalizeSpaces.title: Normalize Spaces
normalizeSpaces.description: Collapse multiple spaces to a single space

trimLeading.title: Trim Leading Whitespace
trimLeading.description: Remove leading whitespace from target text

trimTrailing.title: Trim Trailing Whitespace
trimTrailing.description: Remove trailing whitespace from target text

doc.overview:
The **Whitespace Correct** tool cleans up whitespace in target translations.
It can collapse repeated spaces, trim leading and trailing whitespace, match
the source's leading/trailing whitespace onto the target, remove zero-width
characters, and remove whitespace after specific punctuation marks. The last
is particularly useful when translating from a space-delimited language into a
non-space-delimited one such as Chinese or Japanese.

The tool operates only on the target text, so a target locale is required, and
only blocks that already have a target for that locale are processed.

correctComma.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a comma (ASCII `,` or full-width `，`)
is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctExclamation.help:
When enabled, whitespace following an exclamation mark (ASCII `!` or
full-width `！`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctFullStop.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a full stop (ASCII `.` or full-width
`．`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctQuestion.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a question mark (ASCII `?` or
full-width `？`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

includeHorizontalWS.help:
When enabled, horizontal tab characters are treated as removable after
punctuation.

includeVerticalWS.help:
When enabled, vertical whitespace — line feeds and carriage returns — is
treated as removable after punctuation.

matchSourceWhitespace.description: Copy the source's leading/trailing whitespace to the target.
matchSourceWhitespace.help:
When enabled, the target's existing leading and trailing whitespace is
stripped and replaced with the source's, so the target mirrors the
source's edge whitespace.

normalizeSpaces.description: Collapse runs of multiple spaces to one.
normalizeSpaces.help: When enabled, consecutive spaces are reduced to a single space.

removeZeroWidthChars.help:
When enabled, zero-width spaces and joiners (and the zero-width no-break
space) are removed from the target.

targetLocale.help:
The locale whose target text is corrected. Required; only blocks with an
existing target for this locale are touched.

trimLeading.description: Remove leading whitespace from the target.
trimLeading.help: When enabled, whitespace at the start of the target text is removed.

trimTrailing.description: Remove trailing whitespace from the target.
trimTrailing.help: When enabled, whitespace at the end of the target text is removed.

processingNotes: Operates on the target text only; a target locale is required and only existing targets are processed.
Punctuation correction removes the whitespace that immediately follows each enabled punctuation mark, limited to the included whitespace types.

examples.title: Normalize spacing for a locale
examples.description: Apply the default whitespace cleanup to a target.

examples.title: Tidy CJK punctuation spacing
examples.description: Remove spaces after punctuation for a non-space-delimited target.
examples.config:
targetLocale: zh-CN
correctFullStop: true
correctComma: true

entries.description: Count the words in each block's source and target text for scoping and pricing.

countInline.title: Count Inline Codes
countInline.description: Include inline code content in word counts

doc.overview:
The **Word Count** tool tallies the number of words in every translatable
block, reporting per-block counts and a document total. It is the standard
way to scope a localization job — how much text needs translating — and to
drive word-based pricing.

Counting runs over the segmented text. By default both the source and the
target locale are counted, so you can compare untranslated versus translated
volume. Inline codes (placeholders, tags, variables) are excluded from the
count unless you opt in, because they are markup rather than translatable
words.

countInline.description: Include inline-code content in the word counts.
countInline.help:
By default, inline codes — placeholders, variables, and tag content
represented as coded text — are **excluded** from the count, since they
are not translatable words. Enable this only when you specifically want
inline-code text reflected in the totals.

countSource.description: Count words in the source text.
countSource.help:
When enabled, the words in each block's **source** segments are counted.
Disable it if you only need target-side volume (for example, to measure
translated output).

countTarget.description: Count words in the target text.
countTarget.help:
When enabled, the words in each block's **target** segments are counted
for every target locale present. On untranslated content the target
count is zero, which makes this a quick way to see remaining work.

limitations: Counting follows the active segmentation; results depend on how the text was segmented.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Target counts are reported per target locale.

examples.title: Count source words only
examples.description: Scope a job by source volume, ignoring any existing translations.

examples.title: Include inline codes
examples.description: Reflect placeholder and tag text in the totals.

entries.description: Check that the source and/or target text of each block is well-formed XML.

checkSource.title: Check Source
checkSource.description: Validate source text for XML well-formedness

checkTarget.title: Check Target
checkTarget.description: Validate target text for XML well-formedness

wrapRoot.title: Wrap in Root Element
wrapRoot.description: Wrap text in a root element before validating

doc.overview:
The **XML Validation** tool checks the source and, optionally, target text of
each translatable block for XML well-formedness and records the result on the
block's properties: a validity flag and, when invalid, an error message
identifying whether the source or target failed and why. Empty text is treated
as valid.

Source validation is on by default. To validate the target as well, enable the
target check and supply a target locale. Because block text is often an XML
fragment rather than a complete document, the tool can wrap the text in a root
element before parsing so a fragment with multiple top-level nodes still
validates.

checkSource.description: Validate source text for XML well-formedness.
checkSource.help:
When enabled, each block's source text is parsed as XML and any
well-formedness error is reported.

checkTarget.description: Validate target text for XML well-formedness.
checkTarget.help:
When enabled, the target translation is parsed as XML in addition to the
source. A target locale must be supplied when this is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: a target locale is required when target checking is enabled

locale.help:
The locale whose translation is validated when target checking is enabled.
Required when `checkTarget` is on; ignored otherwise.

dependsOn.condition: only used when target checking is enabled

wrapRoot.description: Wrap text in a root element before validating.
wrapRoot.help:
When enabled, the text is wrapped in a synthetic root element before
parsing, so a fragment with several top-level nodes (which is not a valid
standalone document) still validates. Disable it to require a single
well-formed document.

limitations: Checks well-formedness only; it does not validate against a DTD or schema.
Empty source or target text is treated as valid.

rules.title: Transformation Rules
rules.description: Regex-based tag and text transformation rules

doc.overview:
The **XSLT Transform** tool applies a sequence of regular-expression
transformation rules to block text. Each rule has a pattern and a replacement
(with `$1`, `$2` backreference support), and the rules are applied in order.
Since Go has no built-in XSLT engine, this provides a lightweight,
regex-based equivalent for tag and text rewriting. It does not execute XSLT
stylesheets; for stylesheet-based transforms use the okapi-bridge transform
step.

By default the rules are applied to source text. When the rules list is
empty, blocks pass through unchanged.

applySource.description: Apply the transformation to source text.
applySource.help:
When enabled, the rules are applied to the source text. If neither source
nor target is set, source is used by default.

applyTarget.description: Apply the transformation to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the rules are applied to the target text of every target
locale present on the block.

passOnOutput.description: Pass the transformed output to subsequent pipeline steps.
passOnOutput.help:
When enabled, the transformation output is forwarded to later steps in
the pipeline.

rules.description: The regex transformation rules to apply, in order.
rules.help:
A list of rules, each with a `pattern` (a regular expression) and a
`replace` string supporting `$1`, `$2` backreferences. Rules are applied
sequentially to the text.

limitations: Transformation is regex-based; full XSLT stylesheets are not executed.
Patterns use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround within the match.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
With no rules configured, blocks pass through unchanged.
